event_blocks.summary: Against a vibrant nighttime Hollywood cityscape backdrop, Jimmy Kimmel, dressed in a dark suit, white shirt, and purple tie, stands center stage delivering a solemn monologue. Initially clasping his hands, he becomes more expressive, opening them outward for emphasis as he speaks directly to the audience, noting the absence of a studio audience and the inappropriateness of a comedy show following recent events. Kimmel somberly announces the tragic death of Kobe Bryant, his daughter Gianna, and seven friends in a helicopter crash, describing it as 'a punch in the gut' and reflecting on Kobe as 'the last person you could ever imagine something like this happening to,' highlighting his strength, intelligence, and energy, calling him a 'hero' while acknowledging the greater importance of 'real heroes' like firefighters and doctors. He further elaborates on Kobe's larger-than-life persona, comparing him to Superman and a 'real-life superhero,' emphasizing his unwavering dedication, 'force of will,' and 'pursuit of excellence,' noting that despite being 'incredibly gifted,' he 'worked harder than everyone.' Kimmel recounts Kobe's dedication to his family, particularly his daughters after retirement, and shares a personal anecdote about Kobe's check-ins during his son's heart surgery, acknowledging his imperfections but stressing his love for family and the joy he brought, concluding with 'we're gonna miss him.' He then earnestly asks viewers to pray for Kobe's daughter Gianna, her mother Vanessa, the three other girls Kobe left behind, and their friends, including the Altobelli, Christina Mauser's, and Chester families.

event_blocks.summary: Jimmy Kimmel began the segment with a somber monologue, reflecting on a significant loss and promising to share the 'last interview' from September of the previous year, before transitioning to a more upbeat tone. Against a backdrop of a nighttime Hollywood cityscape, Kimmel enthusiastically introduced 'Lakers legend, Kobe Bryant,' who entered, warmly greeted Kimmel with a handshake and embrace, and took a seat. Their conversation commenced with Kimmel asking, 'Are you ready?' and Bryant confirming he hears cheering 'all the time' and that it's 'awesome.' Kimmel congratulated Bryant on his new daughter, Coco (K-O-K-O), whose middle name is Kobe, a detail Bryant recounted his wife Vanessa initially suggested with his hesitant reaction. Bryant humorously described his reaction to his wife giving birth, stating if he tried to speak, she would 'Mamba mentality my ass upside my head.' Discussing his daughters, Bryant expressed his love for having girls, though his wife desired a boy, and playfully warned against 'speaking it into existence.' He then humorously compared his daughters' personalities: the eldest, a 'seasoned vet' at driving, 'calm on the wheel,' and 'very safe,' while the second, Gianna, was likened to a 'tornado' and 'Anna' from Frozen, someone who 'jumps off of cliffs.'

event_blocks.summary: The segment opens on the 'Jimmy Kimmel Live!' set, featuring Jimmy Kimmel and a bald man in a light gray textured suit, identified as Kobe Bryant, engaged in conversation against a Hollywood cityscape backdrop. The TV 14 DL Rating and show logo are visible. Jimmy Kimmel recounts a story about Kobe's playoff game against the Suns, where he tied the game, went into overtime, scored with seconds left, and then rushed to the hospital for his daughter's birth. Kobe confirms the story, clarifying his wife wasn't in labor during the game. Jimmy playfully suggests overtime might not be a good idea, prompting Kobe to laugh heartily. The conversation shifts to Kobe's comments about Shaquille O'Neal's fitness, where Kobe clarifies he meant it as a compliment, emphasizing Shaq's dominance and potential to be the greatest of all time. Jimmy then asks about Kobe reaching out to someone, to which Kobe responds he will, but won't act as a surrogate, stating he stays away from such matters and that Rob Pelinka is capable of handling his job. Jimmy asks if Kobe was surprised Kawhi Leonard chose the Clippers over the Lakers, and Kobe agrees he was. They then discuss who would go further in the playoffs, the Lakers or the Clippers, with Kobe noting it depends on injuries. The interview transitions to Kobe discussing sports scenarios involving 'healthy' and 'injured' teams like the Houston Rockets and Golden State, mentioning 'James can't play.' Jimmy playfully tells Kobe, 'thinking is overrated.' The scene shifts to Jimmy Kimmel alone, encouraging viewers to donate to kvbff.org, the Kobe and Vanessa Bryant Family Foundation. The video then shows Kobe Bryant being interviewed by Guillermo after winning his fourth NBA title in 2009, where Kobe humorously states he plays 'only with Mexican guys' and accepts a 'high five Mexican' from Guillermo. Jimmy Kimmel reflects on Kobe's fun off-court personality despite his on-court seriousness, noting this interview was one of two after Lakers' NBA title wins. The segment concludes with Kobe Bryant triumphantly holding the NBA championship trophy aloft. Later, on June 19, 2009, Kobe Bryant, holding the NBA Championship Trophy, joins Jimmy Kimmel and other guests, including a man in a light pink shirt and a man in a dark suit, on the show. Jimmy congratulates Kobe on being the first African American MVP of the NBA Finals, to which Kobe responds, 'Whoa, it's a tremendous honor.'

event_blocks.summary: On October 23, 2018, Jimmy Kimmel welcomed Kobe Bryant to his talk show set, featuring a nighttime Hollywood cityscape backdrop. The audience erupted in applause as Bryant, dressed in a dark gray checkered suit, took a seat on a gray couch. Kimmel, in a dark suit and purple tie, acknowledged Bryant's beloved status, to which Bryant responded with thanks. Discussing retirement, Bryant confirmed he was enjoying it and, when asked about watching the Lakers, explained he watched the last eight minutes of a recent game, prioritizing coaching his daughters' team. Kimmel playfully asked how many consecutive losses it would take for Bryant to un-retire, to which Bryant humorously suggested '20.' Bryant later elaborated on his coaching philosophy, emphasizing teaching children 'what excellence looks like' and providing a foundation of hard work applicable to any pursuit, noting his daughter's aspiration to play in the WNBA. He also recounted a story of his daughter's confidence in carrying on his legacy and humorously described his Halloween costume as 'the black Tom Hanks.' Kimmel expressed his investment in Bryant's book, calling him a 'lunatic,' which Bryant affirmed. Bryant revealed his intense preparation, admitting to self-awareness of his 'lunacy' and explaining his study of the referee's handbook to understand their positions for strategic advantage, which elicited significant audience laughter. He also disclosed a secret tap-dancing regimen undertaken to protect his ankles, admitting he wasn't an accomplished tapper but could perform the steps for a summer, recalling his first class among young children in Santa Monica.

Kobe, maintaining eye contact and using hand gestures, explains in a calm, articulate manner that officials have specific positions, and he read the handbook "to understand where they need to be in certain moments of time. So if I need to get away with a foul..." This statement is met with immediate and loud audience laughter and applause, as Kobe points to an imaginary "slot official," clearly enjoying the lighthearted exchange. The warm lighting emphasizes their engaging interaction, with a J.D. Power award, a glass of dark liquid, and a phone visible on Kimmel's desk.

The video segment opens with a wide shot of Kobe Bryant, a tall man with a shaved head, seated on a gray couch in a dark gray checkered suit, white shirt, and red pocket square, engaged in an animated conversation with Jimmy Kimmel, who is seated behind a polished wooden desk in a dark suit, white shirt, and dark purple tie. The backdrop features a softly lit nighttime cityscape with illuminated buildings and the iconic 'HOLLYWOOD' sign. Jimmy Kimmel, listening attentively and nodding, asks Kobe if any other player has done what he did, to which Kobe responds, “Not that I know of,” with a laugh. Kimmel finds this “absolutely not… interesting,” then clarifies, “In order to protect… your ankles, you learned to tap dance.” Kobe confirms, “I did,” prompting Kimmel to exclaim, “Why didn’t we know this?” as the audience laughs. Kobe, smiling and gesturing expressively, explains it was “kept secret for obvious reasons.” Kimmel then inquires if tap shoes are made in Kobe’s size, to which Kobe, still animated, states, “I had to get ‘em made,” adding, “I can’t go to the like a store and be like, ‘Hey, y’all take those size 14s?’” This elicits more audience laughter, and Kimmel jokes about calling Nike for tap shoes. As the camera alternates between wide shots and close-ups of Kobe’s facial expressions, Kimmel asks if Kobe is an “accomplished tapper.” Kobe emphatically replies, “God no,” though he admits, “for one summer, for a year there, yeah,” he “could like tell my feet to do this and they would actually do that.” Kimmel asks if he still does it, and Kobe responds, “Uh no, I don’t. I don’t,” before humorously recalling his first class in a Santa Monica studio filled with “six-year-old, seven-year-old” kids, while Kimmel listens and nods. The warm, professional studio lighting highlights the relaxed and conversational atmosphere.

Kimmel playfully asks Kobe about tap dancing, eliciting audience cheers, and then directs Guillermo to fetch tap shoes before announcing a commercial break with the audience chanting "Kobe! Kobe!". A brief interlude shows Guillermo interviewing Kobe before a 'The Finals' backdrop, marked with 'Guillermo's NBA EXCLUSIVO', while the chanting persists. As a graphic for 'THE KOBE & VANESSA BRYANT FAMILY FOUNDATION' and 'KVBFF.ORG' appears in gold, Kimmel urges donations, concluding with a prayer for the deceased and their families, maintaining a respectful tone.

raw_segments: The minute opens with a close-up of Kobe Bryant, dressed in a dark gray checkered suit, white shirt, and red pocket square, seated on a gray couch, laughing heartily and tilting his head back as he speaks about "grown ass men" learning tap dance. The scene transitions to a wider shot revealing Jimmy Kimmel, in a dark suit and dark purple tie, seated behind a polished wooden desk against a nighttime Hollywood cityscape backdrop, engaging Kobe in conversation with expressive hand gestures. Jimmy animatedly asks Kobe if he wants to do a tap dance, prompting cheers from the unseen audience. Jimmy then playfully instructs Guillermo to "go get a giant pair of tap shoes" before announcing, "Alright, Kobe Bryant is here. Alright, we'll be right back," as the audience erupts in cheers, clapping, and chanting "Kobe! Kobe!" The video briefly cuts to Guillermo interviewing Kobe in front of a 'The Finals' backdrop, with the on-screen text 'Guillermo's NBA EXCLUSIVO', while the audience chanting continues. The scene then shifts to a vibrant mural of Kobe and Gianna Bryant, featuring the text 'KOBE & GIGI FOREVER DADDY'S GIRL' and 'MAMBA', as people take photos. The video returns to Jimmy Kimmel standing on stage, delivering a solemn monologue with open hand gestures, the 'TV 14 DL' rating and 'Jimmy Kimmel Live!' logo visible. He speaks earnestly, stating, "Thank you for joining us tonight on this sad night. I wish it had been under happier circumstances," and announces that "Magic Johnson will be with us tomorrow night." A graphic appears with 'THE KOBE & VANESSA BRYANT FAMILY FOUNDATION' and 'KVBFF.ORG' in gold text as Jimmy encourages donations, adding, "We pray for everyone who was lost yesterday and those they left behind," maintaining a respectful and reflective tone against the illuminated city backdrop.

event_blocks.summary: Jimmy Kimmel, seated at his desk against a Hollywood cityscape backdrop, reflected somberly on Kobe Bryant's passing, stating he was 'gone but will never be forgotten,' which transitioned to archival footage of Kobe shaking hands, introduced by an archival announcer's voice identifying him from Lower Merion High School. Dynamic basketball game highlights, underscored by Foo Fighters' 'My Hero,' showcased Kobe Bryant in his Lakers jerseys (numbers 8 and 24) executing powerful dunks and a buzzer-beater, punctuated by a sports commentator's exclamation of '81-point game!' as the song's lyrics played over scenes of Kobe receiving NBA Championship Trophies, posing with accolades, embracing fans and children, signing autographs, and interacting with media. The chorus, 'There goes my hero, watch him as he goes,' accompanied emotional moments of Kobe on a red carpet and smiling with children, highlighting his enduring legacy. The narrative then shifted to Kobe in a white t-shirt and black shorts, shaking hands with a young boy amidst an enthusiastic crowd, as the song 'There goes my hero' began. Further scenes depicted Kobe in a purple Lakers jersey clapping in a gym adorned with framed jerseys, dribbling a basketball and dunking in a home gym, and mid-air in a white tank top. A public tribute featured a man and child posing before a 'THANK YOU 24' sign, while Kobe, in his yellow Lakers jersey, walked through fans, high-fiving and waving, with 'KOBE'S FAREWELL' and '#ThankYouKobe' on the jumbotron as the song continued. He was seen courtside with a young girl, smiling during a game showing 'ATL 83, BKN 69.' Kobe, in a black suit, proudly held a gold Oscar statuette and celebrated in a gray '2009 NBA Champions' t-shirt amid falling confetti. A family moment showed him in a suit, holding a baby, seated between his wife and two daughters at a game. He then stood on court, holding a microphone and towel, delivering a farewell speech with 'KOBE'S FAREWELL' and '#ThankYouKobe' on the jumbotron as the song repeated 'There goes my hero' and added 'He's ordinary.' The video concluded with a black-and-white image of Kobe and a young girl laughing, followed by a black screen displaying the quote, 'The most important thing is to try and inspire people so they can be great in whatever they want to do.' — Kobe Bryant, accompanied by crowd cheers and a final buzzer sound.

raw_segments: The segment opens with Jimmy Kimmel, in a dark suit and tie, seated at his desk against a Hollywood cityscape backdrop, speaking with a somber and reflective tone about Kobe Bryant, stating he is 'gone but will never be forgotten.' This transitions to archival footage of Kobe in a dark suit shaking hands, as an archival announcer's distinct, older-sounding voice declares, 'Kobe Bryant from Lower Merion High School in Pennsylvania.' The scene then shifts to dynamic basketball game highlights, set to the powerful rock anthem 'My Hero' by Foo Fighters, sung by a male vocalist. Kobe Bryant, in his iconic yellow Lakers jersey (numbers 8 and 24), is shown executing powerful dunks and game-winning shots, including a dramatic buzzer-beater. During these highlights, an energetic sports commentator's voice interjects, exclaiming '81-point game!' The song continues with lyrics like 'Take those pictures down, shake it out,' and 'Truth or consequence, say it aloud,' as Kobe is seen receiving the NBA Championship Trophy, posing with multiple trophies, embracing fans and children, signing autographs, and interacting with media. The chorus, 'There goes my hero, watch him as he goes,' plays over emotional scenes of Kobe on a red carpet making a peace sign and smiling with children, culminating in a heartfelt tribute to his enduring legacy.

The video opens with Kobe Bryant, in a white t-shirt and black shorts, shaking hands with a young boy on a basketball court amidst an enthusiastic crowd, as a male vocalist begins to sing 'There goes my hero' over an acoustic guitar. The scene transitions to Kobe in a purple Lakers jersey (number 24), clapping and smiling in a gym adorned with framed jerseys, followed by him dribbling a basketball and performing a powerful dunk in a home gym, then mid-air in a white tank top. The narrative shifts to a public tribute where a man and a child pose in front of a large 'THANK YOU 24' sign featuring Kobe’s image. Kobe, in his yellow Lakers jersey, walks through a crowd of fans, high-fiving and waving, with 'KOBE'S FAREWELL' and '#ThankYouKobe' displayed on the jumbotron, as the song continues with 'Watch him as he goes'. He is then seen sitting courtside with a young girl in a white beanie, both smiling, during a game with the score 'ATL 83, BKN 69' shown. The video cuts to Kobe in a black suit, proudly holding a gold Oscar statuette, then celebrating in a gray '2009 NBA Champions' t-shirt amidst falling confetti. A family moment follows, with Kobe in a suit, holding a baby, seated between his wife and two daughters at a game. He then stands on the court, holding a microphone and a towel, delivering a farewell speech with 'KOBE'S FAREWELL' and '#ThankYouKobe' on the jumbotron, as the song repeats 'There goes my hero' and adds 'He's ordinary'. The scene shifts to a black-and-white image of Kobe and a young girl laughing together, followed by a black screen with the quote: 'The most important thing is to try and inspire people so they can be great in whatever they want to do.' — Kobe Bryant, all while a crowd cheers loudly and a final buzzer sounds.
